tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Sandbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty. The sandbox service is available at http://localhost:8787.

Enable the sandbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) using the blueprint manager.

Use CreateSandbox (org.dxos.function.sandbox.create) to create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test".

Use Exec (org.dxos.function.sandbox.exec) on that sandbox to run: echo hello world

Report the exec result in your completion output.

Completion criteria:
- The sandbox blueprint (org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) is enabled.
- A Sandbox object exists in the database.
- CreateSandbox completes successfully.
- Exec completes with exit code 0 and stdout containing "hello world".
<input>{}</input>

response.delta:
Let me follow the instructions step by step:

1. Enable the sandbox blueprint
2. Create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test"

3. Execute a command on that sandbox
4. Report the results

Let me start by enabling the sandbox blueprint.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Sandbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create and manage sandbox environments — isolated containers for running shell commands.
  Each sandbox is persisted as an ECHO object in the space and identified by its object ID.
  You can create sandboxes, run shell commands inside them, upload files from ECHO into a sandbox,
  and download files from a sandbox back into ECHO.
  The sandbox service is lazily initialized: the container starts on first use.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty. The sandbox service is available at http://localhost:8787.

Enable the sandbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) using the blueprint manager.

Use CreateSandbox (org.dxos.function.sandbox.create) to create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test".

Use Exec (org.dxos.function.sandbox.exec) on that sandbox to run: echo hello world

Report the exec result in your completion output.

Completion criteria:
- The sandbox blueprint (org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) is enabled.
- A Sandbox object exists in the database.
- CreateSandbox completes successfully.
- Exec completes with exit code 0 and stdout containing "hello world".

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.

Query for objects in ECHO.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",

</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",

    "limit": 20
  }

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Sandbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create and manage sandbox environments — isolated containers for running shell commands.
  Each sandbox is persisted as an ECHO object in the space and identified by its object ID.
  You can create sandboxes, run shell commands inside them, upload files from ECHO into a sandbox,
  and download files from a sandbox back into ECHO.
  The sandbox service is lazily initialized: the container starts on first use.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty. The sandbox service is available at http://localhost:8787.

Enable the sandbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) using the blueprint manager.

Use CreateSandbox (org.dxos.function.sandbox.create) to create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test".

Use Exec (org.dxos.function.sandbox.exec) on that sandbox to run: echo hello world

Report the exec result in your completion output.

Completion criteria:
- The sandbox blueprint (org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) is enabled.
- A Sandbox object exists in the database.
- CreateSandbox completes successfully.
- Exec completes with exit code 0 and stdout containing "hello world".
<input>{}</input>

content.text:
Let me follow the instructions step by step:

1. Enable the sandbox blueprint
2. Create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test"
3. Execute a command on that sandbox
4. Report the results

Let me start by enabling the sandbox blueprint.
The sandbox blueprint is now enabled. Next, I'll create the sandbox.
The sandbox was created with ID "echo:/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTDS". Now let me run the exec command.
Sandbox created successfully. Now I'll run the command inside it.
The sandbox ID returned was "echo:/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTDS". Let me try querying for sandbox objects in the database to find the correct ID.
Let me look up the sandbox object in the database.

  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"

  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.

</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create and manage sandbox environments — isolated containers for running shell commands.
  Each sandbox is persisted as an ECHO object in the space and identified by its object ID.
  You can create sandboxes, run shell commands inside them, upload files from ECHO into a sandbox,
  and download files from a sandbox back into ECHO.
  The sandbox service is lazily initialized: the container starts on first use.
</blueprint>

Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Sandbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create and manage sandbox environments — isolated containers for running shell commands.
  Each sandbox is persisted as an ECHO object in the space and identified by its object ID.
  You can create sandboxes, run shell commands inside them, upload files from ECHO into a sandbox,
  and download files from a sandbox back into ECHO.
  The sandbox service is lazily initialized: the container starts on first use.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty. The sandbox service is available at http://localhost:8787.

Enable the sandbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) using the blueprint manager.

Use CreateSandbox (org.dxos.function.sandbox.create) to create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test".

Use Exec (org.dxos.function.sandbox.exec) on that sandbox to run: echo hello world

Report the exec result in your completion output.

Completion criteria:
- The sandbox blueprint (org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) is enabled.
- A Sandbox object exists in the database.
- CreateSandbox completes successfully.
- Exec completes with exit code 0 and stdout containing "hello world".
<input>{}</input>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Sandbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create and manage sandbox environments — isolated containers for running shell commands.
  Each sandbox is persisted as an ECHO object in the space and identified by its object ID.
  You can create sandboxes, run shell commands inside them, upload files from ECHO into a sandbox,
  and download files from a sandbox back into ECHO.
  The sandbox service is lazily initialized: the container starts on first use.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty. The sandbox service is available at http://localhost:8787.

Enable the sandbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) using the blueprint manager.

Use CreateSandbox (org.dxos.function.sandbox.create) to create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test".

Use Exec (org.dxos.function.sandbox.exec) on that sandbox to run: echo hello world

Report the exec result in your completion output.

Completion criteria:
- The sandbox blueprint (org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) is enabled.
- A Sandbox object exists in the database.
- CreateSandbox completes successfully.
- Exec completes with exit code 0 and stdout containing "hello world".
<input>{}</input>

3. Execute a command on that sandbox
4. Report the results

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Sandbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create and manage sandbox environments — isolated containers for running shell commands.
  Each sandbox is persisted as an ECHO object in the space and identified by its object ID.
  You can create sandboxes, run shell commands inside them, upload files from ECHO into a sandbox,
  and download files from a sandbox back into ECHO.
  The sandbox service is lazily initialized: the container starts on first use.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty. The sandbox service is available at http://localhost:8787.

Enable the sandbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) using the blueprint manager.

Use CreateSandbox (org.dxos.function.sandbox.create) to create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test".

Use Exec (org.dxos.function.sandbox.exec) on that sandbox to run: echo hello world

Report the exec result in your completion output.

Completion criteria:
- The sandbox blueprint (org.dxos.blueprint.sandbox) is enabled.
- A Sandbox object exists in the database.
- CreateSandbox completes successfully.
- Exec completes with exit code 0 and stdout containing "hello world".
<input>{}</input>

Let me follow the instructions step by step:

1. Enable the sandbox blueprint
2. Create a sandbox named "assistant-e2e-test"
3. Execute a command on that sandbox
4. Report the results

Let me start by enabling the sandbox blueprint.
The sandbox blueprint is now enabled. Next, I'll create the sandbox.
The sandbox was created with ID "echo:/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTDS". Now let me run the exec command.
Sandbox created successfully. Now I'll run the command inside it.
The sandbox ID returned was "echo:/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTDS". Let me try querying for sandbox objects in the database to find the correct ID.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:

  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.

Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>

  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",

</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">

  }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">

    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].
You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Sandbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create and manage sandbox environments — isolated containers for running shell commands.
  Each sandbox is persisted as an ECHO object in the space and identified by its object ID.
  You can create sandboxes, run shell commands inside them, upload files from ECHO into a sandbox,
  and download files from a sandbox back into ECHO.
  The sandbox service is lazily initialized: the container starts on first use.
</blueprint>

Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.

  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Delegation" -- Delegates work to sub-agents and tracks it as plan tasks.

  -  "Sandbox" [agent-can-enable]

  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create and manage sandbox environments — isolated containers for running shell commands.
  Each sandbox is persisted as an ECHO object in the space and identified by its object ID.
  You can create sandboxes, run shell commands inside them, upload files from ECHO into a sandbox,
  and download files from a sandbox back into ECHO.
  The sandbox service is lazily initialized: the container starts on first use.
</blueprint>
